author: @ThankYouESM
text: I remember there was an ad around 2005 that showed people riding a stationary bike fitted with a flywheel designed to produce electricity... and has a table fitted for us to even power our laptops on and potentially recharge whatever other devices. It still seems like an awesome invention, but... it was apparently considered far too expensive for their primary clients, whereas... most of those people didn't even own a cellphone. This technology can drastically improve their lives in a whole lot of ways, but... each of those will cost at least $1000 even when the flywheels can be sent empty to be filled with sand/mud; That's $1000 x... "250 million people".

author: @mooneym.3642
text: I need something that can store energy when the sun is down and the PV arrays aren't working. I have got about 15Kva of PV arrays on my farm here in a remoter place than can be imagined. 10KW of that energy is quite often running a large water pump for irrigation. The rest I need for air conditioning, refrigeration, gadgets and lights. Just to give an idea of the scale I often find myself trying to magnify Rob's genius ideas into. About a decade ago there was a paper showing how a university teacher and their students came up with a battery that uses scrap iron and bronze (copper). I would have built that but I could not confirm what diy material would serve as a proper ion exchange membrane, and the university also went silent despite their indication that they will share more info on that battery.

author: @guymee
text:
Hey Robert I have a question and I haven’t found a good answer anywhere I was curious if you know anything about this or anyone else here


So recently I came into a metric crapton of 70% isopropyl based hand sanitizer

And after watching your videos and wanting to build a smokeless indoor heater for my garage, I went to attempt that and obviously I got quite a bit of smoke and a pretty room filling smell from burning it in the garage

Ventilated enough but not ideally I would say


So my intention was to pull the other ingredients out using salt and stirring it around, it’s a very liquid consistency not particularly gel-like, and the ingredients are as follows:

Medicinal ingredient: Isopropyl alcohol 70%

Non medicinal ingredients: Aqua, glycerin, and hydrogen peroxide


I tried it once before and a friend of mine said to add aluminium foil to it to act as a catalyst, I haven’t looked anything up I just thought about how you might have some insight on how exactly to separate the isopropyl from the other ingredients, especially since I have so much it would probably be smart to condense as much of it as I can into it’s purest form so I can then use it as the cleanest burning, hopefully nearly soot free indoor heater

Though I’m not familiar with it’s possible output of carbon monoxide with anything present, I know I still have a lot of research to do myself sorry for being so fresh at all of this

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suspect you could just distil it - carefully!

author: @ClownWhisper
text:
One thing that I have pondered regarding all of these energy harvesting devices is what would be the easiest way to get small amounts of energy into a large Bank of batteries would it require a series of capacitors to build up to a high enough voltage to be able to bleed into a 12-volt system is there a better way than that to do it Mount some of these different devices somewhere for example and every time I walk by pull on a rope or spin by hand even like you just did the device then allow it to drain into a battery Bank. My problem is I don't really know how to get it into the battery bank at low voltage is like this and I'm sure that there are a lot of people like me


I lose power here in my house every four months for maybe 6 to 8 hours which isn't really bad because it's Michigan and we get a lot of serious storms but if I had five or six deep cell batteries as a bank even if I had a very small solar panel it would fill up the battery bank and no time. I had a whole big box probably 30 of those yard lights that work up a tiny solar panels I took those and string them all together into one big solar panel and was able to charge 18 650 batteries I can't remember how I did it it was at my last house but it works great I didn't have to keep my battery charger plugged in anymore I just plugged it into the solar panel I had mounted onto my back window
I'm sure it didn't make much of a difference in my power consumption but why use it the power grid if you have something else to plug in right?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: More turns in the coil (necessitating thinner wire), and higher velocity will get you more voltage. You'll probably want a diode or a bridge rectifier to prevent the battery from discharging when it's underpowered as well. Of course you could use a switching power supply, a boost converter, or a joule thief, but that introduces more complexity. Just turn more wire. And be aware that you're trading off current because you're raising the resistance of the coil. I think the commercial solar controllers use something called an MPPT charge controller - maximum point of power transfer. They're good at converting a very low power input into something that can actually go the right direction into the battery. But if you have a generator that can produce some known minimum threshold of power, you can make it a lot simpler.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: charge in parallel discharge in series mate - we did this when we covered fractal capacitors

author: @Buzzhumma
text: So i wonder if the term battery comes from the ancient times were they used a store of energy called a battering ram to break down fortification and gates etc ?

replies.author: @DABrock-author
replies.text:
No, it comes from the use of the term ‘battery’ meaning multiples of similar or identical items. Originally this was in reference to artillery, where multiple guns were organized into ‘batteries’. This meaning is still in use today.

When Volta an the other early electrical experimenters linked multiple electrochemical cells together, it seemed natural to refer to the assembly as a ‘battery’ of cells, and the term stuck, eventually morphing into the generic term for electrical storage devices, whether they consisted of single cells or multiple.

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: @DABrock-author nice one

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think it is the collective noun for 'group of' mate - hence battery chicken and battery farm

author: @jamest.5001
text: The flywheel off a car or truck engines especially, some can be easily stacked, and mounted on a shaft,  a tractor flywheel can be especially heavy, for non diesel tractors, but diesel tractor flywheels are heavy also, if you are concerned about spinning it over 6 k rpm, I'd wrap it with carbon fiber ribbon, and epoxy resin,  wrap it tightly as possible, then pull a vacuum on it to remove any air pockets! A 20 mm thick ring of carbon, would help hold it together at loads of higher stress than without, I'd definitely test it to operation rpm , and then some, and do regular crack testing! The flywheel from a 1990 Chevy s-10 blazer with 4.3L V6 engine weighs about 50lbs, and is basically a flat disc, that can be stacked, drill out the pressure plate bolt holes in all but one, use a long grade-8 bolt to connect them together, I'd definitely use something in the center to concentrically align them before tightening then, give it a spin, looking for runout from balance  or misalignment, it would be easy to  build a 200-400 lb flywheel, it requires the flywheel from a manual transmission, because a automatic flex plate, only weight a couple pounds at best,   but stack about 4 flywheels together, on a 1.5" shaft, with double bearings on each end , use a CVT to spin it up, so the gear ratios gets higher as load goes down, using solar power I'd mount it in a thick steel box, and pull a vacuum on it, if it can't hold a partial vacuum, I'd try back flushing with hydrogen to reduce friction, since helium is used in this situation, hydrogen should work better, , as long as oxygen doesn't enter the container, and and it gets a spark or the bearings heat up and cause ignition,  pure hydrogen should be no problem,  maybe helium  would be better!

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Biggest challenge with diy is balancing it. I'd say the fastest one might get to before being too nervous because of balance issues is 1000-3000 rpm.  Which is still potentually very dangerous.  But you can still take precautions and be aware if potential failure  modes and take steps to mitigate thise risks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would be better balanced that's for sure but we have covered static balancing before - I will do it again

replies.author: @ricksanchez3176
replies.text: I think keeping it at 1k rpm might be a good idea. Even if you were able to balance it. With a lifting weight you just don't know what the composition is, how consistent, or how mindful they were when they cast it. The castestrophic blow apart would worry me at higher RPMs.  It's like using Tee posts or bed frame rails to build something, you just don't know.  I mean for testing the waters, absolutely, but sticking an angle grinder on it to spin it up, even if you can balance it..... my be some holes in just the drywall..if you're lucky.  Very sincerely, if you go getting up to "self" balancing speeds, get a flywheel that you know what is made from, MPI'd, etc.

author: @BillHallProductions
text:
More bodybuilding talk.

Until recently I was trying to follow a high intensity lifting plan. Low volume, high weight lots of rest.

I've often wanted to build a way to harness the energy of weightlifting. Like maybe a generator that you could set the resistance on to make virtual weights by the Lorenz force.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: The thing about lifting weights is you're exerting a lot of force, but not very fast or for very long. You need a way to store that energy and release it over time. You could build a gravity storage device, lift up something big and heavy with a ratchet mechanism, maybe with an adjustable lever to dial in the weight, and extract electric power on the way down. Cardio machines like treadmills, bikes, and ellipticals are a more direct translation to power. In any case, there's a speed component to power, either torque * rpm or force * velocity.

replies.author: @BillHallProductions
replies.text: @DFPercush well I've considered some sort of flywheel and 2 sets of weights and s mechanism to switch between stacks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I can think of a few ways to do it mate - it's a good idea

replies.author: @BillHallProductions
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering thanks. I have several ideas on how it could work. Both mechanical and electrical. 

The hard part is a solution that gives a good experience for the person working out. You'd want constant pressure

author: @madmaveric
text:
I often find myself considering the losses in most systems and how to minimise them. It seems a big factor of the losses are in the act of pushing a magnet though a field. I would love to see Roberts thoughts on this (You briefly hit on it in a previous video which got me thinking).
We always focus on friction (air, bearings) when looking at efficiency but rarely do I see people talking about capture efficiency, and often not in much detail if they do (probably because it is harder to improve/measure than just using better bearings :P)
I read magnetic generation is about 75% to 95% efficient. I guess the question is at what point is it worth putting effort into trying to improve that side of the losses and how do you even go about that. I have no idea how you would measure/test a system for these kind of losses/efficiency (as I suspect most people don't so they ignore that part of the system :P), so any info on this side of the coin would be great.

replies.author: @eone2345
replies.text:
What he did was just a proof of concept.

The real one was more complicated, in simple, is some sort like a motor-generator which spins the mass with motor when storing energy stage and then acts like a generator when discharging them.

The focus on bearing and drag are just some means to improves efficiency because you will reduce lot's of wear and tear which equals to making money on operations and saving money on maintenance and down time for a long run, with that speed.

They do have another improvement on efficiency and way to calculate that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I will give it some thought - cheers

replies.author: @ET_AYY_LMAO
replies.text: I guess in a system like this you want to limit friction on your axle, as it equates directly to open circuit discharge rate. More friction = faster discharge with no load attached.

replies.author: @madmaveric
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Sorry didn't meant to give you more work :P. I believe efficiency increases the closer the fields are, one thing I did consider is that with modern materials/lubricants I was wondering if embedding magnets in low friction disks (PTFE etc) and then just having them sit on each other with a film of lubricant would increase efficiency enough to overcome the friction added by the mating surfaces/oil. Or maybe a second outer ring of magnets acting as a maglev to maintain a smaller frictionless gap. I suspect the added complication isn't worth it for home projects though.

replies.author: @madmaveric
replies.text: @ET_AYY_LMAO True, Limiting friction/air resistance is the easiest way to improve the system. The other drain on the system (larger than the friction) is the resistance from the generator when a load is put on it. This got me thinking about how these could be improved and I realised I haven't really seen much information on this side of things, can this even be improved?. I suspect most generators efficiency is based off the 'friction' of the generator rather than the efficiency of harvesting the magnetic field, ie is there an optimal distance between fields, number of windings etc or is it just linear (any improvement brings a linear increase in drag). My post was the realisation that I don't really know much about this side of the system

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: 1000 rpm for that wheel is nothing. I  had one running at 13800 some years ago. That was the fastest motor I was able to source. Also tried it with a long piece of string wound around the axle which was pulled to get the wheel to spin. Was a metal mounting plate and all metal contruction so it was safe. BTW mounting the magnets to the steel wheel was a good concept. Helps with the strength of the magnetic  field on the open ends of the magnets.

I must admit I did think of gearing up but it was all just a learning exercise of fun. String pull got me 1100 rpm with a long string .

author: @rowgler1
text: My wood splitter uses a flywheel. It's called a Supersplit, it's really quite amazing. It's classified as a kinetic splitter .

replies.author: @cavelvlan25
replies.text: I have one also. I have trouble with it disengaging from failed splits it binds up or side to side, grinds, squeechn etc. Have to Jacki Chan the lever to get it to disengage it. Iv adjusted it to their suggestions. Any suggestions? Other than avoid cross grain woods altogether lol. I love how fast they can be compared to hydraulic. But mine is a pain to operate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice! I didn't know about that mate - like it!

replies.author: @rowgler1
replies.text:
@cavelvlan25 That is odd. Mine is 15 years old and it still has the original belts. You should just tap down the handle and the flywheels will regain momentum. Some some forked logs can take as many as five -six cycles. If it won"t disengage it will wear out the belts. Look at the lock nut that is at the top of the lever, it shouldn't be cranked down too tight. The handle needs some play to be able to reset the cycle mid split. 
You should not have to avoid any log, but there is some direction and order of split technique that helps. Mine is just the J model with the old Subaru and it splits every thing. One thing that can be a problem is if the log rides up over the wedge and flips over backward onto the spline shaft at the end of a cycle and knocks the shaft under the pinion gear. Then you have to shut it down and remove the cap head screws that hold the carriage to the I-beam to release it. It only happened to me twice, then I figured out what happened the second time, no more. I found that adding some screen door springs inside the original springs helps with the return speed. Hope this helps. I think my Supersplit is a miracle of engineering and it's one of my best tools.

replies.author: @cavelvlan25
replies.text: @rowgler1 mine is made by Dr Power equipment.  Possibly not as quality as your powersplit.  I wasnt too impressed with it. Straight grains it works great but anything taking multiple pushes is a hassle

replies.author: @cavelvlan25
replies.text: @rowgler1 ill look into your suggestion nonetheless
